steps.narration: 03:10 page. The daily_revenue job has been joining on the ingestion-day FX rate instead of the order-day rate since release 41.2 shipped on 2026-06-25. Every daily partition from 06-25 through 07-08 is wrong — 14 days. Today (07-09) already runs the fixed code, and its hourly loads keep landing while we work.

state.note: red = stale (bad FX rate baked in) · accent = rebuilt with fixed code

steps.narration: First, vocabulary: a backfill is not "fixing the data" — it is re-running the transformation for a range of logical dates. The pipeline takes execution_date as a parameter; we will invoke it 14 times, once per day, exactly as if each day were running on schedule.

The naive first attempt, shown so you never make it: rerun 06-25 with an append (INSERT INTO revenue SELECT … WHERE ds='2026-06-25'). The partition already holds the bad rows — now it holds bad plus good. row_count doubles: 1.42M → 2.84M, and sum(revenue) doubles with it. An append-based backfill is a corruption multiplier, not a fix.

state.note: append into a non-empty partition = every rerun adds another full copy

steps.narration: Roll that back and switch to the idempotent form: overwrite at partition granularity — INSERT OVERWRITE … PARTITION (ds='2026-06-25'), or in Spark .mode('overwrite') with partitionOverwriteMode=dynamic. Run it once: 1.42M rows. Run it five times: still 1.42M. Idempotence is what makes a 14-day backfill boring instead of terrifying.

state.note: delete-and-replace the partition atomically — reruns are free

steps.narration: Now the range, in waves of 4 with a resource pool cap — the backfill must not starve tonight's production SLAs. Wave 1 rebuilds 06-25 … 06-28. Meanwhile the hourly job keeps appending to today's partition; the two never touch the same ds, because the backfill range ends at yesterday.

Wave 2: through 07-02. Each day-partition is independent, so order does not matter and neither do failures — if the runner dies after day 8, you rerun days 9–14 and the overwrites make the retry free. Contrast that with the append version, where a retry after a partial failure means forensic row archaeology.

Wave 3: through 07-06. Note what the hourly bar has been doing this whole time — landing on schedule, untouched. A well-partitioned table gives you this isolation for free: history is rewritten day by day while the present keeps arriving.

All 14 partitions rebuilt in ~40 minutes. Verify before you declare victory: 06-25 row_count is back to 1.42M (exactly one row per order), and sum(revenue) reconciles against the finance ledger within rounding. A backfill without a verification query is a hope, not an operation.

The mental model that made this possible: execution_date is a parameter, not now(). Each run computes f(code, inputs, logical_date) — the 06-25 run executed today must produce byte-for-byte what it would have produced on 06-25. Any now() or CURRENT_DATE buried in transformation logic silently breaks that contract.

state.note:
run = f(code, inputs, execution_date) — a pure function of its logical date
now() in pipeline logic is a bug factory: it makes reruns produce different answers

steps.narration: Design every pipeline as if the 14-day backfill is coming — because it is. Partition by logical date, overwrite not append, parameterize on execution_date, cap backfill parallelism below production's share. Then a bad release costs one command and 40 minutes, not a week of manual repair.

state.note: the checklist: date-partitioned output · idempotent partition overwrite · execution_date parameter · bounded backfill pool · verification query
